title: The University’s Never-Ending Crisis
author: https://hedgehogreview.com/contributors/benjamin-s-bernard
text:
What indignities those poor magisters suffered at the cathedral school of Paris late in the twelfth century. In these medieval centers of religious learning, early ancestors of the modern university, teachers, known as “masters,” educated clergymen under the authority of a bishop. They were cosmopolitan institutions, but when peripatetic theologians came to a new city, they sometimes faced legal persecution, special taxes, precarious positions, or too much competition. The disorder extended to students’ lives, too. Some found inexperienced masters at the lectern—others studied hard without getting a diploma, or found no master to take responsibility for them. Political and religious authorities soon decided it was time to clean up the mess. Royal (1200) and papal (1215) documents established in Paris a formal community, or universitas, with the right to self-governance and immunity from civil courts, as well as responsibilities including degree standards. The University of Paris was no longer at the mercy of the bishop.

Stability lasted barely a generation. In 1229, a group of drunken students beat up a tavern-keeper after an argument over their bar tab. They returned the next day with reinforcements, trashed the bar, and roughed up several locals. Adding to Parisians’ anger was the fact that the students couldn’t be tried in civil court, and ecclesiastical courts wouldn’t convict them. The queen regent, Blanche of Castile, ordered the city guards to punish these half-clerical hooligans. In protest, theology students organized a walkout that lasted two years. Many masters departed, too, taking their knowledge and methods with them to other parts of France, and even to modern-day Italy and Spain. One former student followed the deteriorating situation closely. Pope Gregory IX concluded the best way to prevent town-gown conflicts was to better codify and protect the university’s internal functioning, which he accomplished with the 1231 bull Parens scientiarum.11xOn the early University of Paris and the 1229 walkout, see Lyse Roy, “Protesting in Paris, Toulouse, and Caen at the End of the Middle Ages: Legal Means and Means of Action,” Student Revolt, City, and Society in Europe: From the Middle Ages to the Present, eds. Pieter Dhondt and Elizabethanne Boran (London, England: Routledge, 2018), 14–26; Spencer E. Young, Scholarly Community at the Early University of Paris: Theologians, Education and Society, 1215–1248 (Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press, 2014), passim; Nathalie Gorochov, Naissance de l’université: Les écoles de Paris d’Innocent III à Thomas d’Aquin (v. 1200–v. 1245) (Paris, France: Honoré Champion, 2012), passim.

As Europe’s population collapsed during the Black Death, plague battered the fledgling universities even as it provided new opportunities. High mortality among established parish clergy favored young graduates who survived and replaced them.22xWilliam J. Courtenay, “The Effect of the Black Death on English Higher Education,” Speculum, vol. 55, no. 4 (October 1980), 696–714, 713. The university hit a stride it maintained until the invention of print in the mid-fifteenth century and its diffusion by 1500—at which point, any “just-asking-questions” troll or pantheistic cosmographer could publish his cockamamie heresies (looking at you, Giordano Bruno). Printing brought upheaval to universities, threatening their public legitimacy and remaking the dynamics of internal trust, too. Regents scrambled to adapt humanistic education to this new technology. Lectures had been particularly useful as a means of transmitting knowledge when manuscript books were rarer, but now that students had access to printed textbooks, the format needed to serve a different purpose. New forms of censorship, such as the Church’s Index of Prohibited Books, policed the bounds of acceptable thought.

Imagine being a university president with Martin Luther on your faculty when he was summoned by Charles V to appear before the Diet at Worms. As it happened, a young theologian who had stepped in as rector at the University of Wittenberg, Philipp Melanchthon, ended up partnering with Luther to shape the new Protestant movement. This devastating split in Christianity brought with it new possibilities for professional development for budding academics. “While the first years of transition were difficult ones,” writes historian Karin Maag, “the next decades saw the development of an extensive network of academies and universities in both Lutheran and Reformed lands.” Every confession soon needed schools.

In their first few centuries, then, universities withstood versions of the recurring shock waves that would disrupt them again and again in the modern age: labor strikes and casualization, pandemics, technological revolution, and ideological polarization. And as the early-modern moment made clear, the last two often go hand in hand. To those of an opposing confession, scholars seem like heretics, while to a critical reading public, university scholars can seem like foolishly dogmatic pedants guarding increasingly peripheral epistemological territory. It would not be the last time universities were obliged to make tactical adjustments to preserve their relevance.

A Broken Contract

Today, American universities are facing mounting pressure to make fresh changes. Public confidence in higher education dropped from 57 percent in 2015 to 36 percent in 2024, a much-discussed Gallup survey found.33xJeffrey M. Jones, “U.S. Public Trust in Higher Ed Rises From Recent Low,” Gallup, July 16, 2025; https://news.gallup.com/poll/692519/public-trust-higher-rises-recent-low.aspx.  The Trump administration has canceled billions of dollars’ worth of research grants and threatened to freeze federal funding for a long list of elite universities, accusing them of failing to curb antisemitism and seeking to uproot their diversity, equity, and inclusion programs. On April 22, 2025, more than six hundred university leaders signed an open letter written by the American Association of Colleges and Universities, decrying “unprecedented government overreach and political interference now endangering American higher education.”44xAmerican Association of Colleges and Universities, “A Call for Constructive Engagement,” April 22, 2025; https://www.aacu.org/newsroom/a-call-for-constructive-engagement.

The letter is powerful, but the tensions it mentions seem “unprecedented” only if one’s frame of reference is limited to post-1989 America. The university’s authority is in question, but not for the first time. Historically, crises of trust have emerged when epistemic authority, media ecology, and funding in society fall out of alignment—and this is such a time. Luckily, universities are remarkably durable institutions, designed to be rebuilt in place.

In an oft-repeated observation, University of California Chancellor Clark Kerr pointed out that seventy of the “eighty-five institutions in the Western world established by 1520” that “still exist in recognizable forms” are universities.55xClark Kerr, The Uses of the University (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 2001), 115. Cited in David F. Labaree, A Perfect Mess: The Unlikely Ascendancy of American Higher Education (Chicago, IL: University of Chicago Press, 2017), 3; also cited in James Axtell, Wisdom’s Workshop: The Rise of the Modern University (Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, 2016), 2. But it takes a lot of work to look this timeless. Trappings of history such as academic regalia, an “invented tradition” codified in the neo-medieval fads of the nineteenth century, belie how much universities have actually transformed. And change does not always come easy: Looking back at the history of the humanities, scholars Chad Wellmon and Paul Reitter see not timeless stability but “permanent crisis.”

The price of durability is change. Kerr went so far as to title a book Higher Education Cannot Escape History.66xClark Kerr, Higher Education Cannot Escape History: Issues for the Twenty-First Century (Albany, NY: State University of New York Press, 1994). And as Daniel Coit Gilman, who helped inaugurate a new era in American higher education as the first president of Johns Hopkins University, put it, “Universities easily fall into ruts. Almost every epoch requires a fresh start.”77xDaniel Coit Gilman, “The Johns Hopkins University in its Beginning: An Inaugural Address” [1876]. In University Problems in the United States (New York, NY: The Century Company, 1898), 20. This was partly the self-serving reasoning of a reformer, but Gilman makes a compelling point. Reform is the norm, and that notion should offer us some consolation. It would be nearly as naive to expect universities to remain as they were in 1967 or 1989 as it would to seek to preserve their state as of 1762 or 1789. In each instance, universities had to rearticulate their role in society—what historian Emily Levine termed an “academic social contract.” 

That understanding, in postwar America, began with the following exchange: Universities would conduct autonomous research in the nation’s interest, using federal scientific funding; they would educate young future elites and white-collar workers in politics, law, media, and more; and they would carry out other functions besides. Nearly everyone agrees that today that contract has been broken. But by which party to the agreement, the universities or the public? And what is to be done? 

To answer these questions, I spent the past year conducting strategy and fact-finding research with a committee of senior faculty convened by Yale President Maurie McInnis to study declining trust in higher education and advise her on possible reforms. Drawing on community listening sessions and research into higher-education history and policy, we identified recurring criticisms of elite higher education, including high tuition fees, that have led to mistrust. In a high-cost, high-financial-aid model, which varies by university, students and their families don’t know what they will pay until the dust settles on the admissions process. Admissions policies seem opaque, arbitrary, or unfair. Grade inflation has thrown into question the purpose of the discipline we educators impose. Universities have bureaucratized and corporatized. They offer educational and research products of uneven quality. They depend on free, civil expression yet turn inward with ideological conformity. 

These critiques, taken together, suggest that it is time to renegotiate the implicit agreement between universities and their societies—about what social functions they should perform, about the cost and value of education, and much else. Many of these critiques can be addressed by attending to mission, the Committee on Trust wrote in its report. Not unlike Gregory IX’s 1231 bull, the report suggests that it is partly by reforming internal policies in accordance with a clear sense of mission that trust in the implicit agreement between universities and civil society can be restored.88xJulia Adams and Beverly Gage (co-chairs), Report of the Yale Committee on Trust in Higher Education, April 10, 2026; https://president.yale.edu/sites/default/files/2026-04/Report-of-the-Committee-on-Trust-in-Higher-Education.pdf.  That work has begun: On April 30, Yale revised its mission statement in response to our report.

Sometimes explicit contracts are also at stake. My own university is one of the dozens that have seen federal scientific research grants revoked, reinstated, and confounded over the past two years. But how did it come to have such a lucrative relationship with the US government in the first place? By the fifties, even as many American colleges were flush with GI Bill and federal science funding meant to help the country compete with the Soviets, Yale was in a more dire financial position than we might expect today. President A. Whitney Griswold saw an opportunity to compete with Princeton and Harvard by building new scientific facilities. But he was wary of doing so with federal funds. During the Red Scare, he famously backed out of a federal student loan program that would have required students to sign an anti-communist loyalty oath. Of accepting federal scientific research funding, Griswold supposedly quipped, “We must be sure each time that we can ride the horse and that it bears no trace of Trojan ancestry.”99xBrooks Mather Kelley, Yale: A History (New Haven, CT: Yale University Press, 1974), 439. I thank Penelope Laurans for this reference. Yet, as historian Geoff Kabaservice notes, Griswold’s reservations did not stop him: The share of Yale’s revenue from the government ballooned from 2 percent in 1950 to 28 percent in 1968. When Griswold died of cancer, in 1963, his successor, Kingman Brewster, continued the trend, not without similar reservations. Brewster noted in his 1963 Report of the Yale President:

Changes in political and fiscal policy as well as the rise and fall of popular scientific enthusiasm always carry with them the risk of termination of support. This is a real concern, albeit measurable only by conjecture. 

This point could apply just as easily to Yale’s previous principal sources of funding: alumni donations, endowment revenue, and tuition. Brewster continued:

The counsel of prudence requires two cautions. First, we must not become relatively more dependent upon government as a source of income than are comparable competitive institutions.… The second caution is to be absolutely sure that our commitments to faculty or to research appointees do not exceed our ability to honor them out of our own resources, if government assistance were terminated.1010xKingman Brewster, Report of the Yale President, 1963.

Brewster foresaw our predicament, even if one could argue that his reservations no longer apply, considering how much the scale of scientific research has expanded. By understanding the context in which this “deal” was struck—indeed, now that we can think of it once again as a deal at all—we can have better discussions about why, and in what forms, our nation’s continued investment in research is still vital. 

Questioning Authority

But go back further, before and beyond modern America, and broader questions about public trust in universities arise. Consider one of today’s powerful critiques: that liberal-arts faculty are too ideologically homogeneous. If universities today are responsible for teaching a full range of intellectually valid viewpoints, who decides that range of acceptable views? Normally faculty exercise this power of judgment as part of their academic freedoms. This conservative charge alleges, though, that the self-perpetuating body of the faculty, who select their junior colleagues, has become corrupted; and, so, they fail this task of judgment by excluding the views half of America holds. What good is free speech if there’s nobody present to speak? This criticism could be strengthened by a more robust historical account of when and how such corruption occurred. But humanistic fields such as history, in which narratives have political and ethical implications, are beholden to similar demarcation problems as those seeking to distinguish between science and pseudoscience. Which views are rightly, or necessarily, included in reasonable and scholarly conversation? These debates over what is plausibly true, worthy, or deemed intellectual have so many precedents in the history of universities, in every age.

Consider an extreme example. Should a biology department have a creationist on faculty? At Vassar in 1865, six years after the publication of On the Origin of Species, the answer was: absolutely. In his zoology textbook Natural History: A Manual of Zoölogy, Sanborn Tenney wrote that the study of zoology “is eminently adapted to enlarge our ideas of creation, and of the Great Author of Nature.” By the end of the decade, though, his successor was teaching Darwin. Academics treated evolution as settled science before the public did. The accepted natural-science consensus of 1860 had become “fringed” into pseudoscience by 1925, as historian of science Michael Gordin terms the process of marginalization of disproven beliefs.1111xMichael D. Gordin, On the Fringe: Where Science Meets Pseudoscience (New York, NY: Oxford University Press), 2021. In 1925, Tennessee banned schoolteachers from teaching that man is descended from a lower order of animals; commenting on what came to be known as the Scopes Monkey Trial challenging the ban, the Baltimore-based satirical journalist H.L. Mencken quipped, “Two months ago the town was obscure and happy. Today it is a universal joke.” But it wasn’t funny to those on the fringe.

The idea of demarcation can be helpful for understanding other liberal arts, because the dustbin of intellectual history is filled with quacks with doctorates. The tradition of questioning credentialed, learned authority is ancient; Petrarch’s Invectives and Agrippa von Nettesheim’s De incertitudine et vanitate scientiarum et artium renewed it by impugning Scholastics. But the most well-remembered such critique from Renaissance literature is surely Erasmus’s The Praise of Folly. Writing in the classical genre of encomium, the character Folly is as terrifyingly erudite as she is humorous. But she makes some serious points. As Anthony Grafton notes, Folly criticizes “theologians who confuse their obsessive concern with tiny distinctions between abstract concepts with the study of God.”1212xDesiderius Erasmus, The Praise of Folly, trans. Hoyt Hopewell Hudson with foreword by Anthony Grafton (Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, 2015), x. The character Folly declares:

Even those who specially arrogate to themselves the part and name of wise men cannot conceal me.… Wherefore, since in fact they are…“most foolish,” and yet are eager to seem wise men…shall we not with justice dub them μωροσόφους, “foolosophers”?1313xErasmus, The Praise of Folly, 10.

If Folly spies something of herself in so-called “wise men,” who are we to disagree? She ought to know. Today, we still use the same Greek words (mōros and sophos), in reverse order, to denote those who are half educated: our sophomores.

Like Erasmus, other early-modern commentators assailed the learned vices of these latter-day sophists. Johann Burkhard Mencke (sometimes “Mencken”), in Leipzig, added his own contribution to the genre. Mencke was a minor scholar but an active administrator: He edited a journal of criticism, delivered an oration on the three-hundredth anniversary of his university, and served as rector for multiple years. In the 1710s, he gave a pair of lectures, printed as De Charlataneria Eruditorum (On the Charlatanry of the Learned), in which he chronicled the pedantic flaws of scholars. My undergraduate students relished the satire when our seminar gathered in February, around the Beinecke Library’s copy, to discuss the text. It was the nobodies, Mencke noted, who obsessed over their pretentious titles the most: Clarissimus, Magnificus, and the rest. Obsequious philosophers dedicate their books to a patron they have never met, as a ploy for attention. Pansophists claim to teach all philosophy, having themselves only scratched the surface. Today, as one reads Mencke’s smirking mockery, the chasm between credentials and truth seems to grow wider and wider.

Mencke used thinly veiled pseudonyms and initials so that his Leipzig peers would know exactly whom he was insulting. When his enraged marks sued him, he happily paid their fines out of his considerable inherited wealth. Undepleted by these disbursements, a trust was passed down through the centuries to his descendants. Some immigrated to America, including a cousin named Henry Louis, who, two centuries later, wrote about the charlatanry of creationists in Tennessee.

Quacks, False Sages, and Morosophes

The Leipzig Mencken’s critique was ultimately friendly. “As for…these lectures, let no one suppose from what is ridiculous in them that I am an enemy of genuine learning.”1414xJohann Burkhard Mencken, The Charlatanry of the Learned (De charlataneria eruditorum, 1715), ed. Henry Louis Mencken (New York: NY: Alfred A. Knopf, 1937), 49. Instead, he called on scholars to live up to their titles. But the problems he identified became more pronounced as laypeople caught on. During the Enlightenment, as the world of ideas became ever more public and accessible, the barriers to entry into learned debate were lower than they had ever been. Consider, in Enlightenment Paris, the future diplomat Pierre-Michel Hennin and his friends. All young men in the late 1740s and early 1750s, they attended salons, debated in clubs, started an academic journal, joined a Freemason lodge, attended theater and opera, and wrote to one another for years about what they read and saw. They mocked the religious superstitions of their austere parents and eagerly sought out the latest works by Voltaire or Montesquieu. A formative social-intellectual association began in the neighborhood around the university known as the Latin Quarter, where these men formed a group called les Morosophes, or “foolosophers.”1515xBenjamin S. Bernard, “Administering Morals in the French Enlightenment: Education, Sexuality, and Authority at the Parisian Collège, 1645–1763,” PhD dissertation, Princeton University, 2022.

The public culture into which they plunged constituted a significant challenge to established institutions of epistemic authority, from medicine to—especially—theology. How could credentialed experts maintain the standards of their disciplines when any quack could gain a mass following through cheap pamphlets or public lectures that directly appealed to a broad audience such as these young men, circumventing both expert credentials and print censorship?

They could not, really. This new media public sphere was flooded by cranks and charlatans. Quacks, magicians, and hucksters of false information followed close behind. A new celebrity culture facilitated the influence of those who had more confidence than expertise. Our word mesmerize refers to Franz Anton Mesmer, who toured Europe advocating theories of animal magnetism and hypnosis. The Italian occultist Alessandro Cagliostro rose to prominence at the Versailles court, and then he brought the court down with him in scandal. That “Affair of the Diamond Necklace” torched the reputations of the queen, Marie-Antoinette, and the archbishop of Paris. This was, in the words of historian David A. Bell, a “free-wheeling, muckraking, scandal-mongering, gossipy, celebrity-obsessed, and quite frequently pornographic” sort of society.1616xDavid A. Bell, “Was There an Enlightenment Public Sphere? Reflections on the Historiography,” paper delivered to the symposium on “Structural Transformations of the Public Sphere: Historical and Normative Perspectives.” Princeton University Center for Human Values, April 26–27, 2019. On examination, the so-called Age of Reason seems just as much the age of morosophy and of charlatanry.

One solution to the problem of demarcating true and expert knowledge from the enticing falsehoods of epistemic populism was to break down the barrier between experts and the people—to democratize expert knowledge. If the reading public would have access to the most expert information themselves, they wouldn’t need to distinguish experts from charlatans. This approach was typified by Denis Diderot and Jean le Rond d’Alembert’s Encyclopédie. Many entries resemble YouTube how-to tutorials, explaining technical or artisanal tasks. Many are polemical or argumentative essays. In the entry on collège, which referred to elite classical grammar schools, the author offers a backhanded criticism: Classes go too slowly for the smartest students! Faculty and students should be held to high standards. So far, like Johann Mencken, this author implies that what happens in a collège has value. But then he shows his hand:

[A] young man…leaves the college after ten years—among the most precious years of his life—with a very imperfect knowledge of a dead language…often with impaired health, which is the least of the effects of the corruption of morals…[and] with such a superficial knowledge of religion that he succumbs to the first blasphemous conversation….1717xJean-Baptiste le Rond d’Alembert and Edme-François Mallet, “College [abridged],” trans. Nelly S. Hoyt and Thomas Cassirer, The Encyclopedia of Diderot & d’Alembert Collaborative Translation Project (Ann Arbor, MI: Michigan Publishing, University of Michigan Library, 2003); http://hdl.handle.net/2027/spo.did2222.0000.144 (accessed April 30, 2026). Originally published as “Collège [abridged],” Encyclopédie ou Dictionnaire raisonné des sciences, des arts et des métiers, vol. 3 (Paris, France, 1753), 634–637.

Enlightenment philosophes benefited from such a classical education, which they turned around and rejected; meanwhile, their former professors (and their successors) opposed some of the intellectual and stylistic characteristics of Enlightenment writing. This project, the Encyclopédie, brought unintended consequences for the University of Paris. In 1751, the Abbé Jean-Martin de Prades witnessed his Sorbonne defense censured, with the panel claiming that Prades was an Encyclopédiste.1818xJeffrey D. Burson, The Rise and Fall of Theological Enlightenment: Jean-Martin de Prades and Ideological Polarization in Eighteenth-Century France (Notre Dame, IN: University of Notre Dame Press, 2010). The Archbishop of Paris decried not “foolosophers” per se but faux-sages, false sages.1919xChristophe de Beaumont, Mandement de Monseigneur l’Archevêque de Paris, portant condamnation d’une Thèse soutenue en Sorbonne le 18. Novembre 1751, par Jean-Martin de Prades… (Paris, France: Simon père et fils, 1752), 4. At stake was whether the university could compete with or adapt to new media, literary styles, and ideas; or whether it would lose public interest and trust, overtaken by the manipulative, hyperbolic tracts of a parasocial public sphere.

This rupture created deeper problems. By attacking inherited, credentialed, and institutionalized authority, the philosophes weakened the distinctions that separated expert knowledge from imposture. As historian Antoine Lilti has explained in his lectures on charlatanry at the Collège de France, they worried that to capture attention and persuade the public, scholars and charlatans used the exact same techniques. The Leipzig Mencken had noted: “At the present day, some [professors] do not hesitate to employ the same devices as the…actors in the theaters.” In contrast, two generations later, the Encyclopédie entry for “charlatan,” I think, protests too much: 

One must completely distinguish these people from Physicians whose empiricism is enlightened. Medicine based on real experiences is very respectable; that of the charlatan is worthy only of contempt.2020xLouis de Jaucourt, “Charlatan,” trans. Victoria Meyer, The Encyclopedia of Diderot & d’Alembert Collaborative Translation Project (Ann Arbor, MI: Michigan Publishing, University of Michigan Library, 2013); http://hdl.handle.net/2027/spo.did2222.0002.722 (accessed April 30, 2026). Originally published as “Charlatan,” Encyclopédie ou Dictionnaire raisonné des sciences, des arts et des métiers, vol. 3 (Paris, France: 1753), 208–210.

Johann Mencken had recognized demarcation as a problem endemic to institutionalized knowledge, but the Encyclopédistes wanted to make an easier distinction: lumières (enlightened) versus charlatans. The carefully argued, citation-heavy dissertations of the theologians had been overcome by the pamphlets of public intellectuals manipulating the emotions of their readers. 

In fact, the term morosophe has sometimes been used to refer not to the foolishness of credentialed experts but to the inner wisdom of uncredentialed common men. Rabelais developed precisely this meaning of “foolosophy.” Subsequent commentators even played on the tension between these two meanings. In Donizetti and Romani’s 1832 comic opera, L’elisir d’amore, the huckster “Doctor” Dulcamara tricks a young man into buying his love potion, which turns out to be nothing more than a bottle of cheap Bordeaux. Yet, when the young man downs it, he finds his love interest receptive to his advances. Sometimes, the snake oil really works.

For that reason, to build trust in knowledge, including science, a society may need a careful equilibrium that does not exclude folk understandings completely from expert knowledge. We might add to Emily Levine’s “academic social contract,” then, another Rousseauvian notion: that of an epistemic general will. Transposed from political theory into epistemology, a “general will” of knowledge-producing institutions names the conditions under which epistemic authority becomes legitimated. Knowledge claims that violate widely shared standards of reasoning may be true without commanding trust. Much as some students mistake the “general will” to imply either direct democracy or will of the majority, this concept does not necessarily accept a priori the “wisdom of crowds,” either. Consider an oligarchic society in which expertise, or even literacy, is restricted to a few elites: If they are trusted by the masses, the epistemic general will achieves equilibrium. 

Today, the task of distinguishing knowledge from charlatanry animates numerous controversies over university policy. Consider arguments that “viewpoint diversity” is a scholarly virtue, and one currently lacking. It’s not so easy to decide what constitutes a veritable intellectual tradition and what should be excluded on its merits. Nor is it a simple task to discern political activism from scholarship, another controversial subject in our time. Johann Mencken criticized expertise from the inside, but even consensus within the guild would not guard against populist epistemic revolt. Still, internal critique aimed at preserving epistemic standards can play a role in mitigating external attacks aimed at dissolving them. Trust is earned. To rebuild the connection between judgment and authority, it is necessary to offer, as Mencken and our report do, some healthy self-criticism.

Those who identify with the Enlightenment might position themselves in relation to this history in distinct ways. First, we might, optimistically, defend universities as spaces where Enlightenment values—of free thought and speech, human rights, scientific progress, and social equality—can flourish. That perspective might say: While we may have strayed from America’s founding ideals, we should re-read Montesquieu, Rousseau, Burke, Jefferson, Smith, Mill, and the rest to re-center our democracy in its two-hundred-fiftieth year. This mode of historical engagement is not analogical but propositional: We find civic wisdom in those texts without placing primacy on historicist critique.

But there is a second way of relating to that history. Do scholars today really identify with the victors of that Enlightenment story, with the emergent new order of knowledge? Or would it not make more sense to consider us more closely aligned with the crusty old regime of credentialed knowledge? We could easily tell a tale more like this one: In the seventeenth century, epistemology ran too much through rank, title, and privilege (including that of censorship). The forces of a new media ecosystem proved too powerful to combat. In a similar vein, today’s institutionalized epistemic authorities are being blown out of the water by what Princeton historian of science D. Graham Burnett calls “human-fracking” algorithms, for which today’s academic priesthood has proven no match. Even as we identify with the propositional ideals of the Enlightenment, the structural meta-narrative of the history of truth in the eighteenth century offers a different, more troubling parallel. If we don’t offer something valuable for our age, we become not the Rousseau or Donizetti in that metaphor; instead, we become what the grand siècle poet Nicolas Boileau derided as “wretched college prigs,” academics clinging to the decadent sinecures of a counter-Enlightenment.

Is there a third way? While the meta-narrative of an eighteenth-century media revolution may be structurally analogous to our time, the features of that era and those of our own are not quite the same. Broad and intentional action, clear mission-driven choices, may yet resist what seems like inevitable techno-futurism. Many of our core beliefs may be “fringed” one day, too, so we should approach knowledge production with, always, more epistemic humility. Consider that after the intellectual battles in eighteenth-century France, the university would re-emerge in post-Napoleonic German lands in a new form: with more adaptive and participatory institutions like seminars, and with a new organizing principle in the dedication to research. Our task is similar: to decide how a university committed to truth, expertise, and public purpose can operate in a changing world. After all, the history of universities is characterized not just by their endless renovations but also by their enduring foundations. When the nave starts to flood or the edifice starts to crumble, universities remodel. They often do so with excellent taste.
